skill.name: azure-eventhub-dotnet
skill.description: Guides developers through Azure Event Hubs for .NET, including producers, buffered producers, processors, checkpointing, partition usage, and ASP.NET Core integration.
skill.summary: Build and review Azure Event Hubs .NET producer and consumer workflows.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many Markdown backticks and package installation commands as external command execution, but the skill is documentation-only and contains no executable scripts. The weak cryptography finding at SKILL.md:3 is a false positive because that line is only YAML front matter. No prompt injection, malware intent, credential exfiltration, or hidden automation was found.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Backticks Flagged as Command Execution
low_findings.description: Verdict: false positive for command execution. The flagged locations are Markdown code fences, inline code spans, C# examples, and package installation snippets; the skill does not run commands automatically.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file is Markdown documentation with fenced bash and C# examples. I found no script file, hook, or instruction that executes these snippets without user action.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Detector Match
low_findings.description: Verdict: false positive. SKILL.md:3 starts the YAML description block and does not contain a cryptographic algorithm, key handling, hashing function, or encryption code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced line is only front matter text. No cryptographic API or weak algorithm appears at that location.

content.value_statement: Teams often need correct Event Hubs patterns for high-throughput .NET services. This skill provides focused guidance for producers, processors, checkpointing, partitions, and Azure authentication.
actual_capabilities: Explains EventHubProducerClient and EventHubBufferedProducerClient usage for sending events.
Shows EventProcessorClient patterns for production receiving with checkpointing.
Covers partition IDs, partition keys, and event position selection.
Provides ASP.NET Core dependency injection guidance for Event Hubs clients.
Summarizes Azure RBAC roles needed for sending and receiving.
Outlines EventHubsException handling for transient and service busy errors.
limitations: It is a reference guide, not an executable integration package.
It does not provision Azure Event Hubs or storage resources.
Version information may require review against current Azure SDK releases.
It does not include complete production observability or deployment templates.

use_cases.title: Implement a Producer Service
use_cases.description: Design a .NET service that batches events, uses Azure identity, and sends data to Event Hubs.

use_cases.title: Build a Reliable Consumer
use_cases.description: Create an EventProcessorClient workflow with checkpoint storage, error handlers, and partition-aware processing.

use_cases.title: Review Streaming Architecture
use_cases.description: Check whether a proposed Event Hubs design uses correct clients, partition keys, checkpoints, and retry behavior.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Producer
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the azure-eventhub-dotnet skill to help me create a simple .NET producer for Azure Event Hubs.

prompt_templates.title: Add Production Receiving
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the azure-eventhub-dotnet skill to design an EventProcessorClient consumer with blob checkpointing and error handling.

prompt_templates.title: Tune Partition Behavior
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the azure-eventhub-dotnet skill to review my partition key strategy for ordered customer events.

prompt_templates.title: Review an ASP.NET Core Integration
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the azure-eventhub-dotnet skill to review my ASP.NET Core Event Hubs integration for client lifetime, retries, checkpointing, and RBAC.

output_examples.input: Plan a production Event Hubs receiver in .NET.
output_examples.output: A recommended receiver design using EventProcessorClient, blob checkpoint storage, DefaultAzureCredential, process and error handlers, and a checkpoint interval strategy.

output_examples.input: Explain when to use a buffered producer.
output_examples.output: A comparison of immediate batch sending and buffered sending, including throughput benefits, flush behavior, and failure callback requirements.
A concise assessment of ordering needs, partition hot spot risk, and whether a customer or tenant key is appropriate.

best_practices: Use DefaultAzureCredential and Azure RBAC for production authentication.
Reuse Event Hubs clients instead of creating new clients per request.
Checkpoint after a deliberate event count, time interval, or completed processing unit.
anti_patterns: Do not use EventHubConsumerClient as the main production receiver.
Do not checkpoint before downstream processing is complete.
Do not force all events into one partition unless strict ordering requires it.

faq.question: Does this skill run Azure commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides Markdown guidance and examples for .NET developers.

faq.question: Which Azure SDK package does it focus on?
faq.answer: It focuses on Azure.Messaging.EventHubs and the processor package for checkpointed receiving.

faq.question: Can it help with production consumers?
faq.answer: Yes. It describes EventProcessorClient, blob checkpointing, process handlers, and error handlers.

faq.question: Does it include Azure resource provisioning?
faq.answer: No. It does not create namespaces, hubs, storage accounts, or RBAC assignments.

faq.question: Does it recommend connection strings?
faq.answer: It lists connection strings as an alternative, but recommends DefaultAzureCredential for production.

faq.question: Is it suitable for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The report marks support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
